title: Components
narrativeIntro:
The third region reveals itself not as scattered fragments but as interconnected components. Each one is a self-contained unit — its own template, its own logic, its own appearance. They combine like organs in a living thing, each serving a purpose, all working together.

This is Angular. This is how real applications are built. And this is the architecture of The Drift itself.
narrativeOutro: Your first Angular components. They're simple, but they're modular, composable, and alive. The Drift's third region responds to this — structures that were monolithic start to separate into distinct, glowing components.
phmConnectionNote: Ryland starts building complex systems from modular parts. You learn Angular components.

challenges.title: What Renders
challenges.narrativeHook: A component declares its data. Its template speaks it aloud. What do you hear?
challenges.instructions:
An Angular component has a class property and a template that interpolates it.

Without running it, trace what `{{ status }}` evaluates to and predict what appears in the DOM.
challenges.solution:
// No code changes — this is a prediction challenge.
// The answer is: Status: Online
challenges.predictPrompt:
What text does the

element contain?
predictChoices: Nothing — the component must be registered first

explainGate.prompt: What does {{ }} do in an Angular template, and how is it different from a JavaScript template literal `${}`?

hints: {{ }} in Angular is interpolation — it evaluates the expression inside and renders the result as a string.
status is a class property set to 'Online'. The template renders its value, not the property name.
Angular standalone components don't need to be registered in a module — the standalone: true flag is enough.

challenges.title: Counter Component
challenges.narrativeHook: Two buttons. One number. The simplest component that feels alive.
challenges.instructions:
A `CounterComponent` displays a count and has two buttons. The template is already written — implement the class.

- `count` starts at `0`
- `increment()` adds 1
- `decrement()` subtracts 1

files.name: counter.component.ts

tests.testCode: const c = new CounterComponent(); if (c.count !== 0) throw new Error(`count should start at 0, got ${c.count}`);
const c = new CounterComponent(); c.increment(); if (c.count !== 1) throw new Error(`After increment(), count should be 1, got ${c.count}`);
const c = new CounterComponent(); c.decrement(); if (c.count !== -1) throw new Error(`After decrement(), count should be -1, got ${c.count}`);
const c = new CounterComponent(); c.increment(); c.increment(); c.increment(); if (c.count !== 3) throw new Error(`Three increments should give 3, got ${c.count}`);

hints: The template is already done — you only need to implement the class methods.

challenges.title: Silent Binding
challenges.narrativeHook: The component renders. The alert box appears. But the message is empty — the template and the class are speaking different names.
challenges.instructions:
This component should display an alert message but the `<span>` renders empty.

There is exactly one bug: a property name mismatch between the template and the class. Find it and fix it.

files.name: alert.component.ts

tests.testCode: const c = new AlertComponent(); if (!('message' in c)) throw new Error(`AlertComponent has no 'message' property — check the spelling`);
const c = new AlertComponent(); if (c.message !== 'Reactor pressure nominal') throw new Error(`message should be 'Reactor pressure nominal', got '${c.message}'`);

explainGate.prompt: Why does Angular show an empty string instead of an error when {{ message }} references a property that doesn't exist?

hints: Angular interpolation {{ message }} looks for a property named 'message' on the component class. If it's not found, it renders as empty string — no error.
Compare the template binding name and the class property name very carefully. One character is different.
The class has 'mesage' (missing the 's'). The template expects 'message'. Fix the class property name.

challenges.title: Crew Badge
challenges.narrativeHook: A parent component knows the crew member's name. A child displays it as a badge. Wire the connection.
challenges.instructions:
Complete the `CrewBadgeComponent`:

1. It accepts an `@Input()` called `crewMember` (string, default `''`)
2. It has a getter `badge` that returns the string `'CREW: '` followed by `crewMember` uppercased

Example: if `crewMember` is `'grace'`, `badge` returns `'CREW: GRACE'`.

files.name: crew-badge.component.ts

tests.testCode: const c = new CrewBadgeComponent(); c.crewMember = 'grace'; if (c.badge !== 'CREW: GRACE') throw new Error(`Expected 'CREW: GRACE', got '${c.badge}'`);

tests.description: badge should uppercase the crewMember name
tests.testCode: const c = new CrewBadgeComponent(); c.crewMember = 'ilyukhina'; if (c.badge !== 'CREW: ILYUKHINA') throw new Error(`Expected 'CREW: ILYUKHINA', got '${c.badge}'`);

tests.description: badge should work with default empty crewMember
tests.testCode: const c = new CrewBadgeComponent(); if (c.badge !== 'CREW: ') throw new Error(`Default badge should be 'CREW: ', got '${c.badge}'`);

hints: @Input() declares a property that a parent component can pass a value into via [crewMember]="someValue".
Inside the class, use this.crewMember to access the input value.

challenges.title: Notify the Parent
challenges.narrativeHook: The badge can display. Now it needs to speak back — to tell the parent which crew member was activated.

tests.description: selected should be an EventEmitter with an emit() method
tests.testCode: const c = new CrewBadgeComponent(); if (typeof c.selected !== 'object' || typeof c.selected.emit !== 'function') throw new Error('selected should be an EventEmitter instance with an emit() method');

tests.description: select() should emit the crewMember value
tests.testCode: const c = new CrewBadgeComponent(); c.crewMember = 'grace'; let emitted = null; c.selected.subscribe(v => emitted = v); c.select(); if (emitted !== 'grace') throw new Error(`Expected 'grace' to be emitted, got '${emitted}'`);

tests.description: select() should emit whatever crewMember is currently set to
tests.testCode: const c = new CrewBadgeComponent(); c.crewMember = 'ilyukhina'; let emitted = null; c.selected.subscribe(v => emitted = v); c.select(); if (emitted !== 'ilyukhina') throw new Error(`Expected 'ilyukhina' to be emitted, got '${emitted}'`);

explainGate.prompt: How does @Output() + EventEmitter let a child component communicate upward to its parent?

hints: @Output() marks a property as an outgoing event stream. The parent listens with (selected)="handler($event)" in its template.
EventEmitter is Angular's event bus for a single component boundary. Calling this.selected.emit(value) sends a value up to whoever is listening.
The method only needs one line: this.selected.emit(this.crewMember);
